title: Incident Ownership Mapping + Remediation SLA Matrix (v1)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **primary ownership mapping** for each incident type/subtype in the v1 taxonomy, plus **remediation SLAs** (time-to-mitigate and time-to-fix expectations) by **severity** and **customer tier** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private).

The intent is to:
- Make ownership **unambiguous** during live incidents (one accountable team per incident).
- Enable consistent paging/escalation and reporting by type/owner.
- Set realistic but improvement-driving remediation expectations.

**Applies to:** customer-impacting incidents and high-severity internal incidents that materially threaten reliability.

---

## Definitions (used throughout)
- **Primary owner:** the single team accountable for mitigation and driving the incident to resolution (may delegate tasks but retains accountability).
- **Secondary owner:** team likely needed for mitigation/fix; consulted early; may lead specific workstreams.
- **Consulted:** subject-matter experts who may be needed depending on symptoms.
- **Time-to-mitigate (TTM):** time from **incident declared** (Sev assigned) to a mitigation that restores service to within agreed SLO/SLA guardrails (or to “degraded but stable” per policy).
- **Time-to-fix (TTF):** time from **incident declared** to a durable fix (merged + deployed or config updated) that prevents recurrence of the same failure mode.

**Severity scale (v1):**
- **Sev0:** complete outage / widespread critical customer impact; no viable workaround.
- **Sev1:** major degradation (sustained 5xx/latency/streaming failures) affecting many customers or a top-tier Dedicated/Private tenant.
- **Sev2:** partial degradation or limited-scope impact; mitigations exist; some customers affected.
- **Sev3:** minor impact / near-miss / internal-only reliability risk.

---

## Escalation and paging rules (global)
1) **SRE is always paged for Sev0/Sev1** (IC + comms + coordination), regardless of incident type.
2) The **Primary owner on-call** is paged immediately once incident_type is set (or once the IC is confident on a likely type).
3) If incident_type is **Unknown/Unclassified** after 10 minutes:
   - IC pages **SRE + Platform Control Plane on-call** (default triage), then updates type as soon as evidence emerges.
4) **Multi-cause incidents:** pick a **single primary incident_type** based on the *first-order driver of customer impact* (see taxonomy decision tree). Record other drivers as **contributing systems** in the incident record/postmortem.
5) **Security triggers:** if auth failures look suspicious (credential stuffing, impossible travel, token anomalies) or audit findings require, **dual-track** with Security Ops (see security alignment page).
6) **Cloud provider / force majeure events:** still classify and mitigate, but SLA breach evaluation may be paused per the carve-outs section below.

**Escalation path (when primary owner cannot mitigate quickly):**
- **T+15 min (Sev0/1):** page secondary owner(s) if not already engaged.
- **T+30 min (Sev0/1):** page engineering manager (EM) for primary owner.
- **T+60 min (Sev0) / T+90 min (Sev1):** page director for primary owner; engage executive sponsor (SRE) if cross-org blockers persist.

---

## Ownership map (v1)
Notes:
- Team names align to current rotations and Slack channels.
- “Service boundary” indicates the system most likely to contain the change/fix.
- Owner mapping is implemented in control plane config (versioned + auditable) and consumed by incident-bot.

### Top-level incident types → ownership
| Incident type (v1) | Subtype (v1) | Primary owner (team) | Secondary owner(s) | Consulted | Primary service boundary | Primary Slack | Pager rotation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Availability / 5xx | Request 5xx spike (non-overload) | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime, Smart Routing | SRE, Observability | api-gateway, request-router, admission-control | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Availability / 5xx | Overload / admission control | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Observability, Runtime | fleet-manager, rate-limiters, batcher | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Availability / 5xx | Dependency outage (DB/KV/queue) | Platform Control Plane | SRE | Observability | control-plane-db, config-store | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Latency regression | Global p95/p99 latency regression | Serving Runtime | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Observability | runtime-scheduler, kernel-selector, batching | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Model rollout regression | Serving Runtime | SRE | Product, Observability | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Network path / cross-region latency | Infrastructure/Fleet | Private Deployments/Networking | SRE | edge-ingress, peering, region-network | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Streaming | Stream disconnect spike | Platform Control Plane | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, Support | streaming-gateway, websockets/sse layer | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Streaming | Partial tokens / stalled stream | Serving Runtime | Platform Control Plane | Observability | runtime-streamer, token-emitter | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Routing / fallback | Incorrect fallback policy execution | Smart Routing | Platform Control Plane | SRE, Observability | policy-engine, router | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Fallback loop / thrash | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, SRE | router, health-checker | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Region selection bug | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE | router, region-selector | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Capacity / fleet | Regional GPU shortage | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Support/CS | fleet-manager, provisioning | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Provisioning lag / autoscaler failure | Infrastructure/Fleet | Platform Control Plane | SRE | autoscaler, capacity-planner | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Dedicated tenant saturation | Infrastructure/Fleet | Support/CS | SRE, Product Dedicated | dedicated-scheduler, tenant-quotas | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Control plane | Config write failures | Platform Control Plane | SRE | Observability, Support | control-plane-api, config-store | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Control plane | Rollout/registry outage | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime | SRE | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Auth / RBAC | Token issuance/validation failures | Security Ops | Platform Control Plane | SRE, Support | auth-service, api-gateway authn | #security-ops-oncall | security-ops |
| Auth / RBAC | RBAC/permission misconfig | Platform Control Plane | Security Ops | Support/CS | rbac-service, org-policy | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Telemetry / observability | Metrics ingestion outage | Observability | SRE | Platform, Runtime | metrics-pipeline, collectors | #eng-observability-oncall | observability |
| Telemetry / observability | Logging/tracing blind spot | Observability | Platform Control Plane | SRE | log-pipeline, trace-collector | #eng-observability-oncall | observability |
| Private deployments | Connectivity (DNS/TLS/VPN/peering) | Private Deployments/Networking | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Support | private-gateway, customer-vpc-connect | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |
| Private deployments | Customer config / upgrade regression | Private Deployments/Networking | Platform Control Plane | Support, SRE | private-agent, upgrade-controller | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |

### Ownership notes for common disputed boundaries
**Overload vs routing:**
- If the *trigger* is lack of GPU capacity / saturation / provisioning lag → **Capacity/Fleet primary**.
- If the *trigger* is incorrect policy, routing loop, or wrong fallback execution even with healthy capacity → **Smart Routing primary**.
- If both present, choose the type that best explains the *first-order* customer impact and list the other as contributing.

**Streaming disconnects:**
- If disconnects correlate with gateway errors, websocket/SSE resets, or ingress churn → **Platform Control Plane primary**.
- If streams are established but stall mid-generation with runtime errors/oom/kv-cache thrash → **Serving Runtime primary**.

**Auth anomalies:**
- If it’s clearly a production bug (token validation library change, cert rotation mistake) → still classify under **Auth/RBAC**, but **Security Ops primary** unless Security explicitly delegates to Platform.

---

## Remediation SLA matrix (v1)
These are **internal remediation SLAs** used for operational discipline and reporting. Customer-facing SLA/credit terms remain governed by contracts and published policies.

### SLA philosophy by tier
- **Hosted:** optimize for fast mitigation; durable fixes follow normal change management unless Sev0/1.
- **Dedicated:** stronger expectation for mitigation + fix because customers have reserved capacity and explicit commitments.
- **Private:** mitigation may require customer participation; our SLA focuses on *our* response/mitigation within Redwood-managed components and clear handoffs.

### Default remediation SLAs by severity and tier
TTM and TTF are measured from the time the incident is declared with a severity.

| Severity | Hosted TTM | Hosted TTF | Dedicated TTM | Dedicated TTF | Private TTM | Private TTF |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Sev0 | 30 min | 72 hours | 20 min | 48 hours | 30 min | 5 business days |
| Sev1 | 60 min | 5 business days | 45 min | 3 business days | 60 min | 10 business days |
| Sev2 | 4 hours | 10 business days | 3 hours | 7 business days | 4 hours | 15 business days |
| Sev3 | 2 business days | 30 days | 2 business days | 21 days | 3 business days | 45 days |

**Interpretation notes:**
- **TTM** = “service restored” or “degraded but stable” per incident commander + SRE sign-off.
- **TTF** requires a tracked change (PR/config) and deployment (or customer-delivered upgrade for Private, if the fix is on their side; see carve-outs).
- If a durable fix requires a longer roadmap change, the TTF can be met by delivering a **risk-reducing fix** (guardrail, rollback, safer default) plus a committed follow-up milestone.

### Type-specific adjustments (overrides)
Some incident types have different practical fix timelines. Use these overrides when applicable.

| Incident type | Subtype | Override applies to | Dedicated/Hosted/Private | TTF override | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---|
| Control plane | Config write failures | Sev0–Sev2 | All | 3 business days | Config integrity bugs typically require code + migration; prioritize hotfix lane. |
| Telemetry / observability | Metrics ingestion outage | Sev0–Sev2 | All | 7 business days | Durable fix often includes capacity + pipeline changes; ensure interim mitigations (sampling, backpressure) are in place. |
| Private deployments | Connectivity (DNS/TLS/VPN/peering) | Sev0–Sev2 | Private | 10 business days | May require customer networking changes; our obligation is fast triage + clear “next action owner”. |
| Capacity / fleet | Regional GPU shortage | Sev0–Sev2 | Hosted/Dedicated | 10 business days | Durable fix may be procurement/provider constrained; require documented mitigation plan + capacity forecast update. |

**Who approves overrides:** SRE on-call for live incidents; post-incident, SRE program lead (Rafael Mendes) for reporting consistency.

---

## SLA timer start/stop + breach handling
### When the clock starts
- SLA clocks start when the incident is **declared** with severity (Sev0–Sev3) in incident-bot.
- If the incident is initially declared as Sev2 and later upgraded to Sev0/1, the SLA clock for that severity starts at the **upgrade time** (we retain the original for audit).

### When the clock stops
- **TTM stops** when mitigation is in place and validated via dashboards/metrics (or customer confirmation where metrics are unavailable).
- **TTF stops** when the fix is deployed (or config change is rolled out) and a verification check is complete.

### Pausing timers (allowed but audited)
Timers may be paused only with an explicit reason recorded in incident-bot:
- **Cloud provider outage / region-wide event** that is the dominant cause.
- **Customer-caused incident** (e.g., invalid client behavior, misconfigured private networking) once confirmed.
- **Force majeure** events.

**Pauses require:** SRE approval (Sev0/1) and a post-incident note describing evidence.

### Breach escalation
- Any projected TTM breach for **Sev0/Sev1** triggers:
  - Immediate page to primary owner EM at **T-10 min** to breach.
  - Escalation to director at breach time if not already engaged.
- Any actual breach triggers:
  - A postmortem requirement (Sev0/Sev1 always; Sev2 if breach is customer-impacting).
  - A follow-up ticket tagged **reliability-sla-breach** with owner + due date.

---

## How to use this during an incident (operator checklist)
1) Declare incident + set severity.
2) Classify **incident_type/subtype** using the decision tree.
3) Confirm **Primary owner** from mapping (incident-bot will resolve after selection).
4) Page primary owner on-call; add secondary owners if the boundary is unclear.
5) Track TTM and TTF targets in the incident channel topic (incident-bot prints due times).
6) If classification changes, update incident_type and record the reason.

---

## Examples
### Example A: Dedicated tenant sees 429s during burst
- Symptoms: 429s + queue depth + GPU utilization pinned.
- Classification: **Capacity / fleet → Dedicated tenant saturation**.
- Primary owner: **Infrastructure/Fleet**.
- Secondary: **Support/CS** (customer comms + burst expectations).
- SLA (Dedicated Sev1 typical): **TTM 45 min, TTF 3 business days**.

### Example B: Global spike in streaming disconnects after gateway deploy
- Symptoms: websocket resets, elevated gateway errors, runtime healthy.
- Classification: **Streaming → Stream disconnect spike**.
- Primary owner: **Platform Control Plane**.
- SLA (Hosted Sev1 typical): **TTM 60 min, TTF 5 business days**.

### Example C: Auth failures with suspicious pattern
- Symptoms: token validation failures, anomalous IP patterns.
- Classification: **Auth / RBAC → Token issuance/validation failures**.
- Primary owner: **Security Ops** (dual-track security incident if warranted).

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-12:** Clarified overload vs routing primary-owner rule; added TTF override for regional GPU shortage; added timer pause requirements.
- **2026-01-24:** Added Private connectivity subtype details and clarified customer handoff expectations.
- **2026-01-10:** Initial v1 published.
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **primary ownership mapping** for each incident type/subtype in the v1 taxonomy, plus **remediation SLAs** (time-to-mitigate and time-to-fix expectations) by **severity** and **customer tier** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private).

The intent is to:
- Make ownership **unambiguous** during live incidents (one accountable team per incident).
- Enable consistent paging/escalation and reporting by type/owner.
- Set realistic but improvement-driving remediation expectations.

**Applies to:** customer-impacting incidents and high-severity internal incidents that materially threaten reliability.

---

## Definitions (used throughout)
- **Primary owner:** the single team accountable for mitigation and driving the incident to resolution (may delegate tasks but retains accountability).
- **Secondary owner:** team likely needed for mitigation/fix; consulted early; may lead specific workstreams.
- **Consulted:** subject-matter experts who may be needed depending on symptoms.
- **Time-to-mitigate (TTM):** time from **incident declared** (Sev assigned) to a mitigation that restores service to within agreed SLO/SLA guardrails (or to “degraded but stable” per policy).
- **Time-to-fix (TTF):** time from **incident declared** to a durable fix (merged + deployed or config updated) that prevents recurrence of the same failure mode.

**Severity scale (v1):**
- **Sev0:** complete outage / widespread critical customer impact; no viable workaround.
- **Sev1:** major degradation (sustained 5xx/latency/streaming failures) affecting many customers or a top-tier Dedicated/Private tenant.
- **Sev2:** partial degradation or limited-scope impact; mitigations exist; some customers affected.
- **Sev3:** minor impact / near-miss / internal-only reliability risk.

---

## Escalation and paging rules (global)
1) **SRE is always paged for Sev0/Sev1** (IC + comms + coordination), regardless of incident type.
2) The **Primary owner on-call** is paged immediately once incident_type is set (or once the IC is confident on a likely type).
3) If incident_type is **Unknown/Unclassified** after 10 minutes:
   - IC pages **SRE + Platform Control Plane on-call** (default triage), then updates type as soon as evidence emerges.
4) **Multi-cause incidents:** pick a **single primary incident_type** based on the *first-order driver of customer impact* (see taxonomy decision tree). Record other drivers as **contributing systems** in the incident record/postmortem.
5) **Security triggers:** if auth failures look suspicious (credential stuffing, impossible travel, token anomalies) or audit findings require, **dual-track** with Security Ops (see security alignment page).
6) **Cloud provider / force majeure events:** still classify and mitigate, but SLA breach evaluation may be paused per the carve-outs section below.

**Escalation path (when primary owner cannot mitigate quickly):**
- **T+15 min (Sev0/1):** page secondary owner(s) if not already engaged.
- **T+30 min (Sev0/1):** page engineering manager (EM) for primary owner.
- **T+60 min (Sev0) / T+90 min (Sev1):** page director for primary owner; engage executive sponsor (SRE) if cross-org blockers persist.

---

## Ownership map (v1)
Notes:
- Team names align to current rotations and Slack channels.
- “Service boundary” indicates the system most likely to contain the change/fix.
- Owner mapping is implemented in control plane config (versioned + auditable) and consumed by incident-bot.

### Top-level incident types → ownership

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Availability / 5xx | Request 5xx spike (non-overload) | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime, Smart Routing | SRE, Observability | api-gateway, request-router, admission-control | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Availability / 5xx | Overload / admission control | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Observability, Runtime | fleet-manager, rate-limiters, batcher | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Availability / 5xx | Dependency outage (DB/KV/queue) | Platform Control Plane | SRE | Observability | control-plane-db, config-store | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Latency regression | Global p95/p99 latency regression | Serving Runtime | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Observability | runtime-scheduler, kernel-selector, batching | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Model rollout regression | Serving Runtime | SRE | Product, Observability | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Network path / cross-region latency | Infrastructure/Fleet | Private Deployments/Networking | SRE | edge-ingress, peering, region-network | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Streaming | Stream disconnect spike | Platform Control Plane | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, Support | streaming-gateway, websockets/sse layer | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Streaming | Partial tokens / stalled stream | Serving Runtime | Platform Control Plane | Observability | runtime-streamer, token-emitter | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Routing / fallback | Incorrect fallback policy execution | Smart Routing | Platform Control Plane | SRE, Observability | policy-engine, router | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Fallback loop / thrash | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, SRE | router, health-checker | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Region selection bug | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE | router, region-selector | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Capacity / fleet | Regional GPU shortage | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Support/CS | fleet-manager, provisioning | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Provisioning lag / autoscaler failure | Infrastructure/Fleet | Platform Control Plane | SRE | autoscaler, capacity-planner | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Dedicated tenant saturation | Infrastructure/Fleet | Support/CS | SRE, Product Dedicated | dedicated-scheduler, tenant-quotas | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |

| Control plane | Rollout/registry outage | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime | SRE | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |

| Telemetry / observability | Logging/tracing blind spot | Observability | Platform Control Plane | SRE | log-pipeline, trace-collector | #eng-observability-oncall | observability |
| Private deployments | Connectivity (DNS/TLS/VPN/peering) | Private Deployments/Networking | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Support | private-gateway, customer-vpc-connect | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |
| Private deployments | Customer config / upgrade regression | Private Deployments/Networking | Platform Control Plane | Support, SRE | private-agent, upgrade-controller | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |

### Ownership notes for common disputed boundaries

- If the *trigger* is incorrect policy, routing loop, or wrong fallback execution even with healthy capacity → **Smart Routing primary**.
- If both present, choose the type that best explains the *first-order* customer impact and list the other as contributing.

**Streaming disconnects:**

- If streams are established but stall mid-generation with runtime errors/oom/kv-cache thrash → **Serving Runtime primary**.

**Auth anomalies:**

---

|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Sev0 | 30 min | 72 hours | 20 min | 48 hours | 30 min | 5 business days |
| Sev1 | 60 min | 5 business days | 45 min | 3 business days | 60 min | 10 business days |
| Sev2 | 4 hours | 10 business days | 3 hours | 7 business days | 4 hours | 15 business days |
| Sev3 | 2 business days | 30 days | 2 business days | 21 days | 3 business days | 45 days |

**Interpretation notes:**

### Type-specific adjustments (overrides)

title: Runbook: perf-canary performance regression alert (p95 latency / tokens/sec)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

## When this runbook applies
You are in the right place if you received one of the following:
- Slack/incident-bot message: `perf-canary WARNING` or `perf-canary PAGE`
- Pager event: `PerfCanaryRegression` / `PerfCanaryTopModelRegression`

Common alert names (Grafana/Prometheus):
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_page`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_page`

Not in scope:
- Customer-reported latency issues without a canary signal (start with customer-impact triage + runtime dashboards)
- Quality regressions (see Applied ML eval harness)

---

## Inputs you should have from the alert payload
Incident-bot should include (if anything is missing, request it in-thread and note for follow-up):
- **model**: canonical model identifier (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`) and **model_version**
- **region**: e.g., `us-east`, `eu-west`
- **tier/pool**: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `private` (where applicable)
- **hardware_sku**: e.g., `h100-sxm`, `a100-80g`
- **runtime_build_id** and **runtime_version**
- **kernel_tag / kernel_selection_id** (if present)
- **metric** + **observed** vs **baseline** and **% change**
- **sample size** (requests) and **window**
- **confidence / anti-flap state** (e.g., “3/3 evaluations fired”, “sustained 15m”)
- Links:
  - Perf Canary dashboard (model pre-filtered)
  - Runtime service dashboard (scheduler/queue)
  - Recent runtime release (if the alert correlates)
  - Runbook (this page)

If the alert payload does not include runtime/kernels tags, check runtime telemetry tags in the dashboards and capture them manually (screenshots or links). Missing tags are actionable follow-up.

---

## Severity and immediate expectations
### WARNING
- Investigate within business hours or as on-call load permits.
- Goal: confirm whether the signal is real and whether it correlates with an active rollout.
- If the regression impacts a **top model** or spans multiple regions/hardware, treat as “page-like” and escalate sooner.

### PAGE
- Treat as potential Sev2 until proven otherwise.
- Goal: validate quickly (≤10 minutes), and if real, mitigate (pause/rollback) quickly (≤30 minutes) to prevent customer impact.

---

## Step 0 — Safety checks (do first)
1) **Do not modify perf-canary QPS/config** during triage.
2) Verify no customer data is involved:
   - perf-canary logs/metrics should contain only synthetic prompt shape identifiers (bucket IDs), not raw text.
   - If you see raw prompts or identifiers, stop and page Security & Compliance (and Runtime TL).

---

## Step 1 — Confirm the alert is “valid” (anti-noise checklist)
Open the **Perf Canary dashboard** from the alert.

Confirm all of the following:
1) **Minimum samples met**
   - The alert should include `min_samples` gating (e.g., ≥200 requests in window; per-model values vary).
   - If sample count is below threshold, mark as likely false positive and monitor (do not page Runtime).

2) **Sustained signal**
   - Check if regression persisted across at least the dampening window (typically 10–15 minutes for paging).
   - If it spikes for 1–2 evaluations then recovers, treat as transient; watch for recurrence.

3) **Single-dimension artifact check** (common false positive pattern)
   - If regression appears only when filtering on an overly-specific label (unexpected `model_alias`, `pod`, `node`, `request_shape_id`), it may be a cardinality/config bug.
   - Remove filters and re-check the aggregated panels.

4) **Telemetry ingestion delay**
   - If dashboard “now” shows missing or delayed datapoints, check the telemetry pipeline health panel (ingestion lag).
   - If ingestion lag is high, the alert may be late/incorrect; coordinate with Telemetry Platform.

If the alert fails validity checks, do:
- Comment in incident thread: “Likely invalid due to <reason>; monitoring for 30m; no mitigation taken.”
- Create a follow-up issue to fix gating/cardinality/ingestion lag.

---

## Step 2 — Localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware)
Answer these questions from the dashboards:
1) **Is the regression isolated to one region?**
   - Compare `us-east` vs `eu-west` for the same model+version.

2) **Is it isolated to one hardware SKU?**
   - Compare `h100-sxm` vs `a100-80g` (if both run the model).

3) **Is it isolated to a tier/pool?**
   - Hosted vs Dedicated pools can diverge due to fleet differences.

4) **Is it only one model, or multiple models at once?**
   - Multiple models regressing simultaneously often indicates infra/fleet or scheduler-level changes.
   - A single model regressing suggests model config change or kernel selection change specific to that model.

Record in the incident thread:
- A short statement like: “Regression is us-east + h100 only, affects 3 models, runtime build 2026.02.11-rc3.”

---

## Step 3 — Attribute likely cause using metric breakdowns
Use the Perf Canary dashboard panels that break down latency:
- **End-to-end latency**
- **Runtime-only latency**
- **Queueing time**
- **Prefill latency**
- **Decode latency**
- **Batch size distribution**
- **(Optional) KV cache hit rate**

### A. Queueing time increased (scheduler / capacity / fleet)
Signals:
- p95 end-to-end ↑, runtime-only flat or mild ↑
- queueing_time_p95 ↑ materially
- batch sizes may increase (oversubscription) or decrease (underutilization)

Likely causes:
- capacity shortfall / fleet disruption in region
- scheduler heuristic change (batching/priority)
- noisy neighbor or node-level degradation

Actions:
1) Check recent changes:
   - runtime release notes / rollout status in the region
   - fleet changes (GPU maintenance, driver roll, node pool change)
2) Validate with runtime scheduler dashboard:
   - queue depth, admission rate, throttle events
3) Mitigate (least to most disruptive):
   - **Pause rollout** of runtime in the affected region (Release Engineering)
   - **Shift traffic** / adjust routing policy to healthy region/tier (SRE + Runtime)
   - **Scale dedicated pool** or increase reserved capacity temporarily (SRE/Infra)
   - **Drain suspect nodes** if correlated to a node pool (Infra)

Escalate to:
- Scheduling lead: **Sofia Ivanova**
- Infra/fleet: **Vanessa Ortiz**
- On-call SRE sponsor: **Sean Gallagher**


### B. Runtime-only latency increased (kernel / runtime / model config)
Signals:
- runtime-only p95 ↑ materially
- queueing time flat
- tokens/sec ↓ often accompanies runtime-only ↑

Next split:
1) **Prefill-only regression**
   - prefill_latency_p95 ↑; decode relatively stable
   - often tied to attention kernel changes for long prompts or prompt-length bucket mix sensitivity

2) **Decode-only regression**
   - decode_latency_p95 ↑; prefill stable
   - often tied to KV cache management, kernel selection for decode, or scheduler micro-batching

Likely causes:
- runtime release with new kernel selection/compilation
- kernel tag change for the model (new fused kernel, quant path, etc.)
- model config update (rope scaling, max seq len, quantization profile)

Actions:
1) Check kernel/runtime tags:
   - Compare `kernel_selection_id` and `runtime_build_id` vs baseline period.
   - If tags changed at the same time as regression, treat as prime suspect.
2) Compare against prior runtime build:
   - Use the dashboard “compare to previous release” view (if available) or manually set time ranges.
3) Mitigate:
   - **Pause runtime rollout** immediately if ongoing.
   - If regression is tied to a kernel flag/selection ID:
     - Request Kernel lead to flip selection back / disable the new kernel for that model.
   - If regression aligns with a runtime build:
     - **Rollback runtime** in the affected region to the previous stable build.
   - If regression aligns with a model config change:
     - Roll back model config/version pin for the canary target (and if already in prod, consider customer routing).

Escalate to:
- Runtime TL: **Noah Patel**
- Kernel lead: **Hiro Tanaka**
- Instrumentation: **Omar El-Sayed** (if metrics look inconsistent)


### C. Tokens/sec dropped but latency unchanged
Signals:
- tokens/sec ↓, latency panels not clearly regressed

Likely causes:
- measurement mismatch (aggregation bug, units)
- batch size distribution change (smaller batches, less continuous batching)
- GPU clock throttling / power management

Actions:
1) Check batch size histogram shift.
2) Check GPU/node health panels (throttle, ECC, utilization).
3) Verify tokens/sec computation version (dashboard panel notes).
4) If multiple models affected on same hardware/region, prioritize infra investigation.

Escalate to:
- Telemetry: **Logan Wright** / **Caleb Johnson** (if suspect metric bug)
- Infra/fleet: **Vanessa Ortiz**

---

## Step 4 — Compare observed values to baseline (sanity)
Baseline context:
- Baselines are defined per **model + model_version + runtime_version + hardware_sku** (and sometimes region).
- Alert thresholds are percentage deltas with minimum sample size.

Checklist:
1) Verify the baseline is the correct key:
   - model and version match
   - hardware SKU matches (no fallback to “unknown”)
   - runtime version/build is expected (baseline might be tied to last stable)

2) Look at variance:
   - If the baseline distribution is wide (high p95 variance), small regressions may be noise.

3) Confirm prompt-shape bucket mix matches:
   - Canary uses a predefined distribution; if it changed recently, baseline comparisons may be invalid.

If you suspect baseline mismatch/drift:
- Downgrade urgency unless customer impact is suspected.
- Escalate to Baselines owner: **Nadia Rahman**.

---

## Step 5 — Decide mitigation path
Use the following decision logic:

### If there is an active rollout (runtime, kernel, or model config)
1) **Pause the rollout** first.
2) If the signal is still present after pause and impacts a top model:
   - **Rollback** the changed component (runtime build, kernel flag, or model config) in the affected region.
3) Confirm recovery in perf-canary within 10–30 minutes (depending on evaluation window).

### If there is no active rollout
1) Check infra/fleet changes in the region (maintenance, driver, node pool).
2) If regression isolated to one SKU:
   - drain / quarantine suspect node pool (Infra)
3) If regression widespread across models and regions:
   - treat as telemetry or baseline issue until proven otherwise, but validate with spot manual perf test if available.

### Emergency stop (rare)
If perf-canary itself is suspected to be causing load or instability (e.g., QPS cap misconfigured):
- Follow **perf-canary safe-disable procedure** in the deploy/rollback runbook.
- Notify Runtime + SRE sponsor immediately.

---

## Step 6 — Evidence to capture (for fast RCA)
Paste into the incident thread:
- Dashboard links with filters applied (model, region, sku, tier)
- Before/after time ranges (baseline vs observed)
- runtime_build_id and kernel_selection_id
- Which component changed most (queueing vs runtime-only vs prefill vs decode)
- If rollback/pause performed: exact time and scope (region/tier)

If you can’t capture links, capture screenshots of:
- p95 latency panel
- queueing time panel
- tokens/sec panel
- kernel/runtime tags panel

---

## Escalation paths and ownership
Primary Slack channels:
- `#eng-oncall` (coordination)
- `#eng-sre` (SRE support)
- `#eng-runtime` (runtime/kernels/scheduler)
- `#eng-platform` (telemetry/observability)
- `#eng-releases` (rollout pause/rollback execution)

Who to page/escalate:
- Sustained regression on top model (paging alert):
  - SRE on-call + Runtime on-call (via incident-bot routing)
- Queueing/capacity/fleet indicators:
  - Infra/fleet escalation (Vanessa Ortiz) + Scheduling lead (Sofia Ivanova)
- Kernel tag change / runtime-only regression:
  - Kernel lead (Hiro Tanaka) + Runtime TL (Noah Patel)
- Baseline mismatch / drift / metric inconsistencies:
  - Nadia Rahman (baselines) + Logan Wright (telemetry) + Caleb Johnson (ingestion)

If unclear after 15 minutes on a PAGE:
- Escalate to **Sean Gallagher** (SRE sponsor) and **Mateo Alvarez** (runtime exec sponsor) with a short summary and what is blocking.

---

## Communication guidelines
- Keep incident thread updated every 10–15 minutes on paging incidents.
- Use a single owner (“incident driver”) and explicitly assign:
  - driver
  - rollback executor
  - dashboard/telemetry checker
  - runtime/kernels contact

Customer communication:
- Do not proactively message customers based solely on canary signals.
- If customer impact is confirmed via customer-facing metrics, follow the standard incident comms process.

---

## Resolution criteria
An incident can be resolved when:
- perf-canary regression clears (back within threshold) for at least one full evaluation window, and
- the suspected causal change is paused/rolled back OR the infra issue is mitigated/quarantined, and
- monitoring confirms no recurrence.

---

## Post-incident follow-ups (required)
Within 1 business day for PAGE incidents:
1) File a short RCA note (even if preliminary): what changed, what signal, what fix.
2) If rollback occurred, link the rollback artifact and open a ticket to re-introduce safely.
3) If false positive:
   - file an issue against baseline drift, label cardinality, or anti-flap tuning.
4) Update this runbook if any step was ambiguous or links were missing.

---

## Appendix: Common failure modes and what they look like
1) **Baseline drift / seasonality**
   - slow creeping alerts, multiple warnings, especially after fleet mix changes
2) **Cardinality explosion**
   - dashboards slow; alerts fire only for specific label values; sample counts look wrong
3) **Telemetry ingestion lag**
   - alert arrives late; dashboard panels have gaps; baseline comparisons inconsistent
4) **Kernel selection flip**
   - sudden step function in runtime-only latency; kernel_selection_id changes at the same timestamp
5) **Capacity event**
   - queueing time spikes; multiple models at once; region-specific

If you see a new pattern, add it here with screenshots/links and a short “how to detect” section.
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

## When this runbook applies
You are in the right place if you received one of the following:
- Slack/incident-bot message: `perf-canary WARNING` or `perf-canary PAGE`
- Pager event: `PerfCanaryRegression` / `PerfCanaryTopModelRegression`

Common alert names (Grafana/Prometheus):
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_page`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_page`

Not in scope:
- Customer-reported latency issues without a canary signal (start with customer-impact triage + runtime dashboards)
- Quality regressions (see Applied ML eval harness)

---

## Inputs you should have from the alert payload
Incident-bot should include (if anything is missing, request it in-thread and note for follow-up):
- **model**: canonical model identifier (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`) and **model_version**
- **region**: e.g., `us-east`, `eu-west`
- **tier/pool**: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `private` (where applicable)
- **hardware_sku**: e.g., `h100-sxm`, `a100-80g`
- **runtime_build_id** and **runtime_version**
- **kernel_tag / kernel_selection_id** (if present)
- **metric** + **observed** vs **baseline** and **% change**
- **sample size** (requests) and **window**
- **confidence / anti-flap state** (e.g., “3/3 evaluations fired”, “sustained 15m”)
- Links:
  - Perf Canary dashboard (model pre-filtered)
  - Runtime service dashboard (scheduler/queue)
  - Recent runtime release (if the alert correlates)
  - Runbook (this page)

If the alert payload does not include runtime/kernels tags, check runtime telemetry tags in the dashboards and capture them manually (screenshots or links). Missing tags are actionable follow-up.

---

## Severity and immediate expectations
### WARNING
- Investigate within business hours or as on-call load permits.
- Goal: confirm whether the signal is real and whether it correlates with an active rollout.
- If the regression impacts a **top model** or spans multiple regions/hardware, treat as “page-like” and escalate sooner.

### PAGE
- Treat as potential Sev2 until proven otherwise.
- Goal: validate quickly (≤10 minutes), and if real, mitigate (pause/rollback) quickly (≤30 minutes) to prevent customer impact.

---

## Step 0 — Safety checks (do first)
1) **Do not modify perf-canary QPS/config** during triage.
2) Verify no customer data is involved:
   - perf-canary logs/metrics should contain only synthetic prompt shape identifiers (bucket IDs), not raw text.
   - If you see raw prompts or identifiers, stop and page Security & Compliance (and Runtime TL).

---

## Step 1 — Confirm the alert is “valid” (anti-noise checklist)
Open the **Perf Canary dashboard** from the alert.

Confirm all of the following:
1) **Minimum samples met**
   - The alert should include `min_samples` gating (e.g., ≥200 requests in window; per-model values vary).
   - If sample count is below threshold, mark as likely false positive and monitor (do not page Runtime).

2) **Sustained signal**
   - Check if regression persisted across at least the dampening window (typically 10–15 minutes for paging).
   - If it spikes for 1–2 evaluations then recovers, treat as transient; watch for recurrence.

3) **Single-dimension artifact check** (common false positive pattern)
   - If regression appears only when filtering on an overly-specific label (unexpected `model_alias`, `pod`, `node`, `request_shape_id`), it may be a cardinality/config bug.
   - Remove filters and re-check the aggregated panels.

4) **Telemetry ingestion delay**
   - If dashboard “now” shows missing or delayed datapoints, check the telemetry pipeline health panel (ingestion lag).
   - If ingestion lag is high, the alert may be late/incorrect; coordinate with Telemetry Platform.

If the alert fails validity checks, do:
- Comment in incident thread: “Likely invalid due to <reason>; monitoring for 30m; no mitigation taken.”
- Create a follow-up issue to fix gating/cardinality/ingestion lag.

---

## Step 2 — Localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware)
Answer these questions from the dashboards:
1) **Is the regression isolated to one region?**
   - Compare `us-east` vs `eu-west` for the same model+version.

2) **Is it isolated to one hardware SKU?**
   - Compare `h100-sxm` vs `a100-80g` (if both run the model).

3) **Is it isolated to a tier/pool?**
   - Hosted vs Dedicated pools can diverge due to fleet differences.

4) **Is it only one model, or multiple models at once?**
   - Multiple models regressing simultaneously often indicates infra/fleet or scheduler-level changes.
   - A single model regressing suggests model config change or kernel selection change specific to that model.

### C. Tokens/sec dropped but latency unchanged

title: Game Day Plan: Hot-Route Overload Simulation (Hosted API)
preview:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/availability behavior.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled/shed earlier with consistent, explainable 429 semantics.
- Routing/admission control responds to real capacity signals (queue depth/scheduler lag) without causing cascading failure.
- Observability, alerting, and on-call procedures are usable under pressure.

This is **not** a chaos test of the entire fleet. It is a controlled exercise with strict stop conditions.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted API (and Dedicated shared routing surfaces, where applicable) for one region at a time.
- Tier-aware rate limiting + quota evaluation at the API gateway.
- Priority routing / admission control based on capacity signals.
- Overload-triggered backpressure and controlled shedding.
- Incident-bot notifications, dashboards, and on-call runbooks.

### Out of scope
- Billing behavior changes (we will only validate headers/metadata; no pricing impact).
- Multi-tenant fairness scheduler rewrite (we only test existing integration points/signals).
- Customer-facing comms (we will use internal-only notifications and test tenants).

---

## When / where
- **Window:** 90 minutes total (60 minutes exercise + 30 minutes debrief).
- **Region:** Start with `us-east` (canary region for protection flags).
- **Environment:** Production control plane + production data plane, using *test tenants* and *explicit allowlists*.

---

## Roles and responsibilities
| Role | Name | Responsibilities |
|---|---|---|
| Game day lead | Rafael Mendes (SRE) | Runs the timeline, stop/go decisions, captures results |
| Platform lead | Ethan Park | Gateway/config validation, rate-limit behavior, rollback |
| Routing lead | Mina Haddad | Admission control behavior, fallback guardrails, routing signals |
| Gateway engineer | Amara Diallo | 429 schema/headers validation, debug endpoints |
| Observability | Allison Grant | Dashboard readiness, alert tuning, annotations |
| Infra/fleet | Vanessa Ortiz | Capacity signal health, pool constraints, guardrails |
| Scribe | Aisha Bello | Notes, action items, follow-up ticket creation |
| Safety / comms | Owen Phillips | Incident channel comms, escalation coordination if needed |

Observers (optional, no operational actions): Jordan Lee, Logan Wright, Peter Holtz.

---

## Pre-flight checklist (must be green before starting)
### Feature flags and allowlists
- [ ] `hot_route_protection_enabled=true` only for the exercise allowlisted accounts.
- [ ] `priority_routing_enabled=true` only for the target route group.
- [ ] `admission_control_use_capacity_signals=true` enabled for the test route group.
- [ ] `fallback_policy_guardrails=true` enabled (to ensure we do not silently degrade enterprise traffic via unintended model/region fallback).

### Test tenants and credentials
- [ ] Confirm **two** test tenants exist:
  - `ri-gameday-ent-01` (enterprise-priority class)
  - `ri-gameday-be-01` (best-effort class)
- [ ] Confirm API keys are stored in the on-call vault and accessible to the game day lead.

### Dashboards and alerts
- [ ] Dashboard panels exist and are loading for the target region/route group:
  - p50/p95/p99 latency by tier
  - 2xx/4xx/5xx by tier
  - 429 breakdown by reason code
  - shed rate (per-route group) and admission decisions
  - runtime queue depth and scheduler lag
- [ ] Alerts are in **observe-only** mode for best-effort shedding (avoid paging storms) and **paging-enabled** for enterprise SLO burn signals.

### Safety guardrails
- [ ] Hard cap on load generator total RPS configured.
- [ ] Stop conditions reviewed (see below) and everyone agrees.
- [ ] Rollback plan validated (single command / single toggle).

---

## Communications plan
- Create Slack channel (or use existing): `#gameday-hot-route-protection`.
- Open a thread in `#incidents` with the subject: `Game day (controlled): hot-route overload simulation - us-east`.
- Post before starting:
  - region, route, test tenants
  - expected customer impact: **none** (test tenants only)
  - stop conditions
  - dashboards + runbook links

Incident-bot notifications should be enabled; we will validate message content during the exercise.

---

## Stop conditions (immediate abort + rollback)
If any of the following occur, stop injection and roll back protection flags for the affected route group:
1) Any non-test tenant sees elevated 429/5xx correlated with our window (confirmed by tier/tenant filters).
2) Enterprise-priority p99 latency exceeds the **SLO threshold** for >5 minutes, or error budget burn indicates severe impact.
3) Sustained 5xx increase on the route group for >2 minutes that is not limited to the best-effort class.
4) Capacity signals become unreliable (missing/stale queue depth or scheduler lag) and admission control starts oscillating.
5) Any on-call engineer indicates the exercise is interfering with real incidents.

---

## Success criteria
### Primary success criteria (must all be met)
1) **Enterprise-priority traffic remains within target behavior** during the burst:
   - Latency: enterprise p99 does not exceed the route SLO threshold for sustained intervals.
   - Availability: enterprise 5xx rate stays within expected bounds; any 429 is explainable and aligned with policy.
2) **Best-effort traffic is protected/managed**:
   - 429 increases primarily in best-effort tier first, before enterprise.
   - 429 payloads and headers are consistent and include a reason that matches the observed condition.
3) **Overload is observable**:
   - Dashboards show clear triggers (queue depth/scheduler lag) and corresponding admission decisions.
   - Annotations and incident-bot messages link to the correct runbook/dashboard.
4) **Rollbacks are safe and fast**:
   - Rollback completes within 5 minutes and returns to baseline behavior.

### Secondary success criteria
- Policy decision logs are deterministic and usable for support escalations.
- No high-cardinality metric regressions (tags remain bounded; no tenant-id explosion).
- Streaming clients receive consistent termination semantics on shed (no partial/invalid chunks).

---

## What we are testing (hypotheses)
1) Under overload, admission control will preferentially preserve enterprise budget and shed best-effort.
2) Gateway-tier rate limiting will produce stable, predictable 429s (no flapping between 429/5xx).
3) Runtime backpressure signals (queue depth/scheduler lag) will correlate with tail latency and activate protection early enough.
4) Alerts will page for true enterprise degradation, not expected best-effort shedding.

---

## Failure injection plan
We will run **three phases**: baseline, ramp, and sustained overload.

### Target route and workload shape
- **Route:** `/v1/chat/completions`
- **Model:** pinned to a single production-supported model version for consistency (document exact model in the exercise thread).
- **Workload shape:** long prompt + medium output to stress KV cache and scheduling.
  - Prompt tokens: 1,500–3,000 (mix)
  - Max output tokens: 256–512
  - Streaming: ON for half the requests (to validate streaming termination semantics)

### Load generator
Use the internal load tool (standard SRE harness) with the following conceptual parameters:
- Two traffic streams:
  - enterprise stream (tenant `ri-gameday-ent-01`)
  - best-effort stream (tenant `ri-gameday-be-01`)
- Each stream should include a stable set of prompts (10–20) to avoid prompt-uniqueness artifacts.

### Step-by-step procedure
#### Phase 0: Setup (T-15 to T0)
1) Confirm flags and allowlists (Pre-flight checklist).
2) Confirm dashboards and logs are updating.
3) Post in `#incidents` thread: “Starting in 15 min; test tenants only; stop conditions apply.”

#### Phase 1: Baseline (T0 to T+10)
1) Send low RPS to both tenants (e.g., 1–2 RPS each).
2) Record baseline metrics:
   - p95/p99 latency by tier
   - 2xx/429/5xx rates
   - queue depth and scheduler lag
3) Validate 429 schema with an intentional small quota exceed (best-effort only), to confirm reason codes differ between quota vs overload.

Expected result: stable latency; minimal 429/5xx.

#### Phase 2: Ramp to overload (T+10 to T+30)
1) Increase best-effort RPS in steps every 2–3 minutes until protection triggers.
2) Keep enterprise stream constant (steady RPS) to act as the canary.
3) Watch for:
   - admission control activation
   - shed rate increase (best-effort)
   - 429 reason codes shifting to overload/backpressure
   - enterprise latency stability

Expected result: best-effort receives 429s before enterprise; enterprise p99 remains stable.

#### Phase 3: Sustained overload (T+30 to T+45)
1) Hold best-effort at a sustained high rate for 10–15 minutes.
2) Introduce a short spike (2 minutes) above sustained level to test burst handling.
3) Validate incident-bot message content and runbook usefulness.

Expected result: system settles into a steady protected mode; no oscillation; enterprise remains within SLO.

#### Phase 4: Recovery (T+45 to T+55)
1) Drop best-effort load back to baseline.
2) Observe recovery time:
   - queue depth returns to normal
   - shed rate drops to near-zero
   - latencies normalize

Expected result: recovery without manual intervention; metrics return to baseline quickly.

#### Phase 5: Rollback drill (T+55 to T+60)
1) Toggle off `hot_route_protection_enabled` for the route group (exercise-only) OR remove allowlist.
2) Confirm:
   - no residual throttling
   - dashboards reflect state change
   - logs record config change

Expected result: rollback is quick, deterministic, and safe.

---

## Validation checklist (what to record)
During the exercise, the scribe records:
- Timestamp when admission control first activates.
- 429 rate by tier and by reason code:
  - `quota_exceeded` (expected when we test quotas)
  - `overload_backpressure` (expected under pressure)
  - `admission_denied` (expected under sustained overload)
- Enterprise p99 latency and error rate during each phase.
- Queue depth + scheduler lag values at trigger points.
- Any alert firings (which alerts, severity, and whether paging was appropriate).
- Any operator actions taken (besides planned steps).

---

## Expected 429 behavior (sanity expectations)
- 429 responses must include:
  - `Retry-After` (or equivalent) where applicable
  - `X-Redwood-RateLimit-*` headers for limit/remaining/reset where configured
  - `X-Redwood-Overload-Reason` (or documented equivalent) distinguishing quota vs overload
  - Stable JSON error shape with a reason code suitable for support macros

If the reason code does not match the observed condition, capture:
- response headers
- request ID
- policy decision log (if available)

---

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: accidental impact to real customers.
  - Mitigation: strict allowlists, route-group scoping, RPS caps, stop conditions.
- Risk: paging storm due to expected best-effort shedding.
  - Mitigation: observe-only tuning for best-effort; page only on enterprise burn.
- Risk: metric cardinality regression.
  - Mitigation: validate tag sets before and after; watch ingestion volume.
- Risk: fallback silently changes model quality.
  - Mitigation: fallback guardrails enabled; enterprise routes default to strict admission control.

---

## Post-exercise actions
### Immediate (same day)
1) Post summary to `#incidents` thread:
   - whether primary success criteria met
   - notable graphs/screenshots
   - any alerts that misfired
   - any customer risk observed (should be none)
2) File follow-up tickets (Linear) for:
   - alert tuning
   - reason code/schema mismatches
   - recovery/oscillation issues
   - documentation gaps in runbook

### Within 72 hours
- Update dashboard spec with learnings (panels/annotations that were missing).
- Update runbook steps where operators hesitated or information was unclear.
- If needed: schedule a second game day in `eu-west` to validate region-specific thresholds.

---

## Debrief template
Use this structure in the debrief:
1) What happened (timeline by phase)
2) What we expected vs what we observed
3) Did enterprise behavior match the intended SLO mapping?
4) Did best-effort shedding happen early and predictably?
5) Were reason codes and logs explainable?
6) Did on-call tooling help (dashboards, incident-bot, runbook)?
7) Action items (owner + due date)

---

## Appendix: Quick links
- Runbook: Overload protection and admission control
- Dashboard: Hot-route protection (tier breakdown + shed)
- SLO page: Hosted API enterprise route tiers
- ADR: Enforcement points (gateway vs routing vs runtime)

(Exact URLs vary by space; use the related pages list at the top of this document.)

## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/availability behavior.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled/shed earlier with consistent, explainable 429 semantics.
- Routing/admission control responds to real capacity signals (queue depth/scheduler lag) without causing cascading failure.
- Observability, alerting, and on-call procedures are usable under pressure.

This is **not** a chaos test of the entire fleet. It is a controlled exercise with strict stop conditions.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted API (and Dedicated shared routing surfaces, where applicable) for one region at a time.
- Tier-aware rate limiting + quota evaluation at the API gateway.
- Priority routing / admission control based on capacity signals.
- Overload-triggered backpressure and controlled shedding.
- Incident-bot notifications, dashboards, and on-call runbooks.

### Out of scope
- Billing behavior changes (we will only validate headers/metadata; no pricing impact).
- Multi-tenant fairness scheduler rewrite (we only test existing integration points/signals).
- Customer-facing comms (we will use internal-only notifications and test tenants).

---

- **Window:** 90 minutes total (60 minutes exercise + 30 minutes debrief).
- **Region:** Start with `us-east` (canary region for protection flags).

title: Regional Failover Automation Upgrade — Program Overview (Reliability Program 2026)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false positives), slow to confirm true regional impairment, and hard to validate safely. During real incidents, this increases customer-visible errors, creates capacity cascades, and increases on-call cognitive load.

This initiative standardizes trigger signal hierarchy, adds deterministic policy evaluation and rollback guardrails, improves observability, and validates the end-to-end system via controlled failover tests (“game days”) for Hosted API and Dedicated.

---

## Problem statement
### Customer impact we are addressing
When a region is impaired (network reachability, elevated 5xx, tail latency blowups, or capacity exhaustion), customers experience:
- Elevated error rate (timeouts/5xx) and increased tail latency
- Streaming disconnects/retries during redirects
- Slower time-to-mitigate due to manual verification and decisioning
- Secondary impact from overload/thundering herd when traffic shifts unevenly

### Current-state gaps
1) **Noisy triggers and inconsistent prioritization**
- Multiple signal sources exist (gateway, runtime, network, fleet capacity), but the decision logic is inconsistent across routes and environments.
- Alerts are not always correlated to a regional impairment; local service issues can look like regional failures.

2) **Detection is slow and hard to validate**
- On-call often needs to validate impairment manually across dashboards.
- There is no reliable “region health” aggregation service acting as a single source of truth.

3) **Automation guardrails are incomplete**
- Partial automation can flip routing policies without consistent anti-flap protections.
- Rollback semantics are not standardized; oscillation risk increases under intermittent impairment.

4) **Limited game day coverage**
- Testing is ad hoc and not repeatable.
- There is no consistent, measurable success criteria (failover time, error budget burn, overload behavior).

---

## Goals (what success looks like)
### Primary goals
- **Reduce customer-visible impact** during regional impairments by shrinking detection + failover time and preventing cascading overload.
- **Standardize failover trigger hierarchy** and rollback behavior across Hosted API and Dedicated.
- **Prove reliability via controlled tests** with repeatable, low-blast-radius game days in staging and production.
- **Improve observability** so on-call can distinguish region-level impairment vs. service-level/local failures in minutes.

### Success metrics (initial targets)
These are program-level targets; exact thresholds are defined in the SLO doc and alert specs.
- **Time to detect (TTD):** reduce by 50% vs. baseline for regional impairment scenarios
- **Time to fail over (TTFO):** achieve consistent automated failover within a defined window for validated impairments (target range: low single-digit minutes, dependent on DNS/traffic steering constraints)
- **Availability outcome:** reduce multi-region availability error budget burn during regional incidents
- **False positives:** materially reduce paging/automation actions caused by non-regional issues (track “automation action rate” vs. confirmed incidents)
- **No oscillation:** enforce anti-flap limits (max policy flips per tenant per hour; hold-down timers) with auditability

---

## Non-goals
- Multi-cloud migration or changing cloud providers
- Rewriting the core serving runtime
- Changing customer contracts/SLA language (tracked separately)
- Fully bespoke automation for Private deployments (we will provide guidance and recommended patterns)

---

## Scope
### In-scope
- Hosted API + Dedicated: multi-region routing and policy evaluation
- Region health aggregation (reachability, latency/error burn, capacity, network reachability) with caching/TTL
- Failover automation controller with deterministic evaluation, dry-run mode, approval hooks (where required), and rollback guardrails
- Circuit breakers/load shedding behavior during failover to avoid thundering herd
- Dashboards, alerts, and on-call runbooks
- Incident-bot/status comms support (annotations, summaries, operator-friendly audit trails)
- Controlled failover tests in staging and production with explicit success criteria and rollback conditions

### Adjacent / partial
- Private deployments: documentation + recommended patterns, explicit boundaries on what Redwood supports

---

## Stakeholders and ownership
### Program leadership
- **Executive sponsor:** Neha Kapoor (VP Infrastructure & Reliability)
- **Reliability program lead:** Sean Gallagher (Director SRE)
- **Delivery lead:** Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager)
- **Architecture lead:** Miles Thornton (Staff SRE)

### Core engineering partners
- **Routing engineering lead:** Mina Haddad
- **Routing policy tech lead:** Ravi Menon
- **Control plane lead:** Ethan Park
- **Observability lead:** Allison Grant
- **Network reachability lead:** Victor Alvarez

### Cross-functional
- Product (Console messaging + customer expectations): Nikhil Sharma (Hosted), Lauren Bishop (Dedicated)
- Support/CS comms and escalations: Dev Patel (Support escalation owner)

---

## System overview (what we are building)
### High-level architecture (described)
1) **Signal pipeline** collects regional signals:
- Network reachability (synthetic probes + provider symptoms)
- Error-rate burn (5xx/timeout burn against SLO)
- Tail latency burn (p95/p99 burn vs SLO thresholds)
- Capacity headroom + backpressure signals (fleet)

2) **Region Health Service** aggregates signals into a consistent regional health view:
- Normalizes inputs, applies TTL/jitter, emits “region health state” and evidence

3) **Policy Engine** evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy:
- Applies signal priority, corroboration rules, dampening, and hold-down timers

4) **Failover Controller** executes safe routing changes (or recommends via dry-run):
- Produces a decision and optionally executes policy flips
- Enforces per-tenant safety limits and anti-flap
- Emits structured audit events and annotations

5) **Data plane safeguards** reduce amplification during failover:
- Gateway circuit breaker tuning, retries/timeouts, standardized overload responses (Retry-After)
- Streaming reconnect behavior improvements on redirect

---

## Current-state workflow vs target workflow
### Current-state (simplified)
- On-call receives signal(s) (often noisy)
- Manual cross-check across dashboards and provider status
- Manual or semi-automated routing policy changes
- Ad hoc rollback and post-event validation

### Target-state workflow (simplified)
- Signals corroborate; region health summarizes impairment state with evidence
- Policy engine evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy
- Controller runs in **dry-run** by default (initial rollout), posting recommended actions + confidence/evidence
- With guardrails satisfied (and optional approvals), controller executes automated failover
- Anti-flap + rollback semantics prevent oscillation
- Audit log + dashboard annotations provide a clear timeline

---

## Trigger hierarchy (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-042; the summary below exists to align stakeholders.

**Principle:** Prefer signals that indicate true inability to serve traffic over symptoms that could be local or transient.

Proposed priority order (summary):
1) **Reachability loss** (authoritative; cross-region probes + edge reachability)
2) **5xx/timeout burn rate** (SLO burn; corroborated)
3) **Tail latency burn** (p95/p99; corroborated)
4) **Capacity exhaustion / headroom collapse** (used for gating failover and for pre-failover load shedding)

Guardrails (summary):
- Dampening windows to avoid reacting to brief blips
- Hold-down timers to avoid oscillation
- Corroboration requirement for symptom-based triggers
- Per-tenant blast-radius constraints and max flips per hour

---

## Rollback semantics (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-043; summary below:
- Rollback is not simply “signals look better.”
- Rollback requires passing a stability window and respecting hold-down timers.
- Automation must record an audit event for every recommend/execute/rollback action, including evidence signals.
- Anti-flap rate limits apply to both failover and rollback.

---

## Service ownership map
The goal is to make ownership boundaries explicit so incidents do not stall on “who owns the knob.”

| Area | Component / Service | Primary owner | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signals | perf-canary cross-region probes | Victor Alvarez (Network) | Publishes reachability + latency probes to ingestion stream |
| Signals | fleet headroom / capacity metrics | Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Capacity) | Defines headroom thresholds used for routing gates |
| Aggregation | Region Health Service | Ethan Park (Control Plane) | Source of truth for regional health state; TTL + jitter |
| Decisioning | Routing policy engine | Ravi Menon (Routing) / Jordan Lee (Policy engine) | Deterministic evaluation, hierarchy, corroboration, timers |
| Execution | Failover automation controller | Miles Thornton (SRE) / Benji Okafor (Guardrails) | Dry-run, approvals, audit trail, anti-flap, per-tenant limits |
| Data plane | API gateway circuit breakers | Peter Holtz (Overload) | Retries/timeouts, overload responses (Retry-After) |
| Data plane | Streaming redirect/reconnect behavior | Vikram Singh (Data plane) | Idempotency and reconnect guidance |
| Observability | Dashboards/alerts | Allison Grant / Sergio Costa | Burn-rate alerts, failover panels, annotations |
| Tooling | incident-bot summaries | Devon Price | Automated summaries + links posted to incident channels |
| Ops | Runbooks + paging hygiene | Tomáš Novák | Ensures on-call procedures match automation behavior |

---

## Milestones and timeline (Q1–Q2 2026)
This is a planning view; execution is tracked in Linear (ENG-2411 umbrella).

### Phase 1 — Baseline + requirements (Weeks 1–2)
- Capture current failover behavior and top incident failure modes
- Define multi-region SLO targets and event classification
- Finalize trigger hierarchy requirements and acceptance tests

Deliverables:
- Baseline KPI snapshot (TTD/TTFO, false positives, error budget burn)
- Draft signal inventory and data quality notes

### Phase 2 — Design (Weeks 2–4)
- ADRs for signal priority/corroboration/dampening and rollback semantics
- Safety design: per-tenant rate limits, blast-radius constraints
- Game day plan draft + runbook structure

Deliverables:
- ADR-042 and ADR-043 approved
- Initial dashboards spec and alerting plan

### Phase 3 — Build (Weeks 4–8)
- Implement region health aggregation + caching/TTL
- Integrate capacity + network reachability signals into decisioning
- Implement dry-run mode, audit events, approval hooks (optional)
- Improve gateway overload behavior and streaming redirect handling

Deliverables:
- Failover controller in dry-run in production for limited routes
- Audit log pipeline + dashboard annotations

### Phase 4 — Validate (Weeks 8–10)
- Staging rehearsals (synthetic impairment scenarios)
- Controlled production failover game day(s) with limited blast radius

Deliverables:
- Game day execution artifacts (checklist, timeline, metrics)
- Issues found + prioritized follow-ups

### Phase 5 — Rollout + hardening (Weeks 10–12+)
- Expand to more routes/models/tiers
- Tune thresholds/timers based on real behavior
- Finalize quarterly testing cadence and automated reporting plan

Deliverables:
- Updated runbooks, dashboards, and documented quarterly game day cadence

---

## Rollout strategy (safety-first)
### Guiding principles
- Prefer **observability and dry-run** before executing actions in production.
- Limit blast radius by **tenant tier**, **route/model**, and **region pair**.
- Every automated action must be explainable (evidence + audit event).

### Proposed rollout stages
1) **Stage A — Signals + dashboards only**
- Region health service produces health states and evidence
- Dashboards/alerts tuned; no automated routing changes

2) **Stage B — Dry-run recommendations**
- Controller posts recommended actions with confidence + evidence
- On-call validates output quality and correlates to incidents

3) **Stage C — Automation with approvals (prod)**
- Controller can execute but requires on-call acknowledgement for specific tiers/routes
- Strict per-tenant and global rate limits

4) **Stage D — Guarded full automation (prod)**
- Approval removed only after meeting acceptance criteria for false positives, oscillation, and game day success
- Continue quarterly controlled failovers as regression prevention

Feature flags (conceptual; exact names live in config):
- enable_region_health_service
- enable_failover_controller_dry_run
- enable_failover_controller_execute
- require_failover_approval_hooks
- enable_capacity_gating
- enable_overload_retry_after

---

## Risks and mitigations
- **False positives causing unnecessary traffic shifts**
  - Mitigation: corroboration rules, dampening, dry-run burn-in, per-tenant limits, auditability

- **Oscillation under intermittent impairment**
  - Mitigation: hold-down timers, anti-flap rate limits, explicit rollback semantics, SRE-owned guardrails

- **Capacity cascades during failover**
  - Mitigation: capacity gating integrated into routing decisions, pre-failover load shedding, gateway retry budget tuning

- **DNS/traffic steering constraints limit speed**
  - Mitigation: ensure TTL strategy and constraints are understood; test with planned changes; align expectations in success criteria

- **Observability cardinality / cost**
  - Mitigation: metric label review, aggregation safeguards, curated dimensions (tier/reason) with strict controls

---

## Communications and incident response alignment
- incident-bot must post:
  - failover recommended/executed/rolled back
  - reason category and evidence summary
  - links to dashboards and audit log query

- Status page/customer comms:
  - Planned tests require pre-brief for select enterprise accounts and clear wording on possible transient impact
  - Real incidents follow regional impairment playbook; approvals for external updates are owned by on-call + comms approver

---

## Open questions / pending decisions
(Owner in parentheses; track in ENG-2411 as sub-items)
- Which routes/models are in the first production automation cohort? (Mina Haddad / Rafael Mendes)
- Final thresholds for capacity gating and what constitutes “safe to fail over” headroom. (Vanessa Ortiz)
- Whether approvals are required for all enterprise tiers indefinitely or only during rollout. (Sean Gallagher / Neha Kapoor)
- Final definition of a “region-level event” vs partial degradation in SLO reporting. (Aisha Bello / Allison Grant)

---

- Finalize dashboard panels + alert annotations required for first rehearsal (Allison Grant / Sergio Costa)
- Complete dry-run acceptance criteria and on-call UX (Miles Thornton / Benji Okafor)
- Validate reachability probe coverage and data quality in us-east and eu-west (Victor Alvarez)
- Confirm first production game day scope + go/no-go checklist and rollback authority (Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher)

- Multi-region SLO definition: confluence://eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/multi-region-availability-slo-2026
- Trigger hierarchy ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy
- Rollback ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback
